subject: Physics
q: The maximum and minimum amplitude of an amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The modulation index for this amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______. (Round off your answer to the nearest integer)
question: The maximum and minimum amplitude of an amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The modulation index for this amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______. (Round off your answer to the nearest integer)
solution:
Modulation index =Amax-AminAmax+Amin

=16-816+8=824=13=0.33

x×10-2=0.33

So, $x=33$

subject: Physics
q: The maximum and minimum voltage of an amplitude modulated signal are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The percentage modulation index will be
question: The maximum and minimum voltage of an amplitude modulated signal are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The percentage modulation index will be
solution:
If amplitude of carrier wave is Ac and amplitude of message wave is Am,

Amax=Ac+Am and Amin=Ac-Am

Modulation index,

Mi=AmAc=Amax-Amin2Amax+Amin2=60-2060+20=12=50%

subject: Physics
q: In amplitude modulation, the message signal Vm(t)=10sin2π×105t volts and carrier signal VCt=20sin2π×107t volts. The modulated signal now contains the message signal with lower side band and upper side band frequency, therefore the bandwidth of modulated signal is α kHz. The value of α is:
question: In amplitude modulation, the message signal Vm(t)=10sin2π×105t volts and carrier signal VCt=20sin2π×107t volts. The modulated signal now contains the message signal with lower side band and upper side band frequency, therefore the bandwidth of modulated signal is α kHz. The value of α is:
solution:
Bandwidth =2×fm

=2×105 Hz=200 kHz

What should nearly be the minimum height of receiving antenna to receive the signals in line of sight ? (Assume radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
question: Two identical antennas mounted on identical towers are separated from each other by a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. What should nearly be the minimum height of receiving antenna to receive the signals in line of sight ? (Assume radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
solution:
D=22Rh

h=D28R=4528×6400 km≅39.55 m

subject: Physics
q: A TV transmission tower has a height of <math><mn>140</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> and the height of the receiving antenna is <math><mn>40</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What is the maximum distance upto which signals can be broadcasted from this tower in LOS (Line of Sight) mode? (Given: radius of earth <math><mo>=</mo><mn>6.4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A TV transmission tower has a height of <math><mn>140</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> and the height of the receiving antenna is <math><mn>40</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What is the maximum distance upto which signals can be broadcasted from this tower in LOS (Line of Sight) mode? (Given: radius of earth <math><mo>=</mo><mn>6.4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
D=2hTR+2hRR

D=2×140×64×105+2×40×64×105

D=8×103[28+8]

D=8× 10 3 [ 2 7 +2 2 ]

D=16× 10 3 [ 7+ 2 ]

D=16×1032.6+1.4

=64000m=64km≃65km

subject: Physics
q: The maximum amplitude for an amplitude modulated wave is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> while the minimum amplitude is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The modulation index is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
question: The maximum amplitude for an amplitude modulated wave is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> while the minimum amplitude is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The modulation index is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
Amax=Ac+Am=12

Amin=Ac-Am=3

⇒Ac=152 and  Am=92

Modulation index =AmAc=92152=0.6

⇒x=1

subject: Physics
q: The number of amplitude modulated broadcast stations that can be accommodated in a $300 \mathrm{kHz}$ band width for the highest modulating frequency $15 \mathrm{kHz}$ will be:
question: The number of amplitude modulated broadcast stations that can be accommodated in a $300 \mathrm{kHz}$ band width for the highest modulating frequency $15 \mathrm{kHz}$ will be:
solution:
Given, modulating frequency $f_m$ $=15 \mathrm{KHz}$
$\therefore \quad$ Bandwidth of one channel $=2 f_m=30 \mathrm{kHz}$
$\therefore \quad$ No of channels accommodate
$$
=\frac{300 \mathrm{kHz}}{30 \mathrm{kHz}}=10
$$

subject: Physics
q: The TV transmission tower at a particular station has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. For doubling the coverage of its range, the height of the tower should be increased by
question: The TV transmission tower at a particular station has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. For doubling the coverage of its range, the height of the tower should be increased by
solution:
Maximum distance covered is given by d=2Rh, here R is the radius.

Now, to increase the length of radio transmission to 2 times,2d=2h'R

Height of tower is h'=4h=4×125=500 m

\(\begin{aligned} \Delta H=h^{\prime}-h &=500-125 \\ &=375 \end{aligned}\)

subject: Physics
q: An amplitude modulated signal is given by $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{t})=10[1+0.3$ $\left.\cos \left(2.2 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{t}\right)\right] \sin \left(5.5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{t}\right) .$ Here $\mathrm{t}$ is in seconds. The sideband frequencies (in $\mathrm{kHz}$ ) are, [Given $\pi=22 / 7$ ]
question: An amplitude modulated signal is given by $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{t})=10[1+0.3$ $\left.\cos \left(2.2 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{t}\right)\right] \sin \left(5.5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{t}\right) .$ Here $\mathrm{t}$ is in seconds. The sideband frequencies (in $\mathrm{kHz}$ ) are, [Given $\pi=22 / 7$ ]
solution:
Equation given
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{t})=10\left[1+0.3 \cos \left(2.2 \times 10^{4}\right)\right] \\
\sin \left(5.5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{t}\right) \\
=10+1.5\left[\sin \left(57.2 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{t}\right)+\sin \left(52.8 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{t}\right)\right] \\
\omega_{\mathrm{c}}+\omega_{\mathrm{w}}=57.2 \times 10^{4}=2 \pi \mathrm{f}_{1}
\end{array}
$$
$$
\mathrm{f}_{1}=\frac{57.2 \times 10^{4}}{2 \times\left(\frac{22}{7}\right)}=9.1 \times 10^{4} \simeq 91 \mathrm{KHz}
$$
$$
\omega_{\mathrm{c}}-\omega_{\mathrm{w}}=52.8 \times 10^{4}
$$
$$
\mathrm{f}_{2}=\frac{52.8 \times 10^{4}}{2 \times\left(\frac{22}{7}\right)} \simeq 84 \mathrm{KHz}
$$

Upper side band frequency $\left(f_{1}\right)$ is
$$
\mathrm{f}_{1}=\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{c}}-\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{w}}=\frac{52.8 \times 10^{4}}{2 \pi} \approx 85.00 \mathrm{kHz}
$$
Lower side band frequency $\left(\mathrm{f}_{2}\right)$ is
$$
\mathrm{f}_{2}=\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{c}}+\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{w}}=\frac{57.2 \times 10^{4}}{2 \pi} \approx 90.00 \mathrm{kHz}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
The amplitude of 15 sin(1000 πt) is modulated by 10 sin(4 πt) signal. The amplitude modulated signal contains frequencies of

A. 500 Hz

B. 2 Hz

C. 250 Hz

D. 498 Hz

E. 502 Hz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
The amplitude of 15 sin(1000 πt) is modulated by 10 sin(4 πt) signal. The amplitude modulated signal contains frequencies of

A. 500 Hz

B. 2 Hz

C. 250 Hz

D. 498 Hz

E. 502 Hz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
The carrier wave frequency is given by

fc=1000π2π= 500 Hz

The modulating wave frequency is given by

fm=4π2π= 2 Hz

Hence, the frequencies contained in the modulated signal are given by

f=fc-fm,fm,fc+fm=498 Hz, 500 Hz, 502 Hz

subject: Physics
q: To radiate EM signal of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> with high efficiency, the antennas should have a minimum size equal to:
question: To radiate EM signal of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> with high efficiency, the antennas should have a minimum size equal to:
solution: For efficient radiation and reception, the height of transmitting and receiving antennas should be comparable to a quarter of wavelength of the frequency used. So for efficient transmission of signals of wavelengths λ the minimum length of antenna should be λ4.

subject: Physics
q: The required height of a TV tower which can cover the population of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn></math> lakh is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If the average population density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo></math>per square <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and the radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: The required height of a TV tower which can cover the population of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn></math> lakh is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If the average population density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo></math>per square <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and the radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Maximum range of TV transmission d=2Rh

d=2×6400×h×10-3 (here, h in m)

Maximum area of TV transmission

Area =πd2

=π×2×6400×h×10-3 km2

Now, 6.03×100000=100×π×2×6400×10-3 h

h=6.03×10510×π×128

h=150 m

subject: Physics
q: The modulation frequency of an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> radio station is <math><mn>250</mn><mi>k</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>,</mo></math> which is <math><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></math> of the carrier wave. If another <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> station approaches you for license what broadcast frequency will you allot?
question: The modulation frequency of an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> radio station is <math><mn>250</mn><mi>k</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>,</mo></math> which is <math><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></math> of the carrier wave. If another <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> station approaches you for license what broadcast frequency will you allot?
solution:
Carrier wave frequency =250×10=2500 kHz

Frequency used 2500±250k Ω=2250 kHz to 2750 kHz

The allotted frequency showed be such that f±250 kHz should not overlap with previous one. Hence possible frequency =2000 kHz

subject: Physics
q:
If the height of transmitting and receiving antennas are 80 m each, the maximum line of sight distance will be :

Given : Earth’s radius =6.4×106 m.
question:
If the height of transmitting and receiving antennas are 80 m each, the maximum line of sight distance will be :

Given : Earth’s radius =6.4×106 m.
solution:
Given: R=6.4×106 m

Using the relation between the range of the radio communication and the height of the transmission antenna hT and the receiving antenna hR, the maximum line of sight distance between two antennas, is dmax=2RhT+2RhR

⇒dmax=2×2×6.4×106×80=64000 m=64 km

subject: Physics
q: A carrier wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>160</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> volts is made to vary between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>max</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>min</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> by a message signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> volts. The peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub></math> of the modulating signal is,
question: A carrier wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>160</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> volts is made to vary between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>max</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>min</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> by a message signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> volts. The peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub></math> of the modulating signal is,
solution:
Maximum amplitude, 
Amax=Am+AC
⇒Vmax=Vm+VC
200=Vm+160
Vm=40
∴ Peak voltage Am=40
Answer is 40.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statement is NOT correct?
question: Which of the following statement is NOT correct?
options: Ground wave signals are more stable than the sky wave signals.
The critical frequency of an ionospheric layer is the highest frequency that will be reflected back by the layer when it is vertically incident.
Electromagnetic waves of frequencies higher than about $30 \mathrm{MHz}$ cannot penetrate the ionosphere.
Sky wave signals in the broadcast frequency range are stronger at night than in the day time.
solution: Above critical frequency $\left(f_c\right)$, an electromagnetic wave penetrates the ionosphere and is not reflected by it.

subject: Physics
q: Only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the optical source frequency is the available channel bandwidth for an optical communicating system operating at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. If an audio signal requires a bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi>kHz</mi></math>, how many channels can be accommodated for transmission
question: Only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the optical source frequency is the available channel bandwidth for an optical communicating system operating at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. If an audio signal requires a bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi>kHz</mi></math>, how many channels can be accommodated for transmission
solution:
Frequency f=cλ=3×1081000×10-9=3×1014 Hz

Band width used for transmission =2100×3×1014 Hz=6×1012 Hz

Number of channels N=Total bandwidth of channel Bandwidth needed per channel =6×10128×103=6008×107=75×107

subject: Physics
q: A carrier wave of peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used for transmitting a message signal. The peak voltage of the modulating signal given to achieve a modulation index of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>%</mo></math> will be:
question: A carrier wave of peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used for transmitting a message signal. The peak voltage of the modulating signal given to achieve a modulation index of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>%</mo></math> will be:
solution: Modulation index, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>m</mi></msub><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>c</mi></msub></mfrac></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>m</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0.8</mn><mi> </mi><mo>×</mo><mn>14</mn></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>.2</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>

subject: Physics
q: A carrier wave of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is modulated by a sinusoidal base band signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The ratio of maximum amplitude to minimum amplitude in an amplitude modulated wave is
question: A carrier wave of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is modulated by a sinusoidal base band signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The ratio of maximum amplitude to minimum amplitude in an amplitude modulated wave is
solution:
The given data is 

Ac=15 VAm=3 V

Thus, the maximum amplitude and minimum amplitude is 

Amax=Ac+AmAmin=Ac-Am

Hence, the ratio is 

AmaxAmin=15+315-3=1812=32

subject: Physics
q: An amplitude-modulated wave is represented by, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>m</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>cos</mi><mn>12560</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>111</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The modulating frequency in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kHz</mi></math> will be
question: An amplitude-modulated wave is represented by, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>m</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>cos</mi><mn>12560</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>111</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The modulating frequency in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kHz</mi></math> will be
solution:
The function of the amplitude-modulated wave is represented by, Cm=AC1+AmACsin2πfmt, now compare it from the given equation, 
Cm(t)=10(1+0.2cos12560t)sin111×104t V

⇒2πfm=12560 s-1⇒fm=2000 Hz=2 kHz.

subject: Physics
q: A radar has a power of $1 \mathrm{~Kw}$ and is operating at a frequency of $10 \mathrm{~GHz}$. It is located on a mountain top of height $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The maximum distance upto which it can detect object located on the surface of the earth (Radius of earth $=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$ ) is
question: A radar has a power of $1 \mathrm{~Kw}$ and is operating at a frequency of $10 \mathrm{~GHz}$. It is located on a mountain top of height $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The maximum distance upto which it can detect object located on the surface of the earth (Radius of earth $=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$ ) is
solution: Maximum distance on earth where object can be detected is $\mathrm{d}$, then $\begin{array}{ll} & (h+R)^2=d^2+R^2 \\ \Rightarrow & d^2=h^2+2 R h \\ \text { since } & h < < R, \quad \Rightarrow d^2=2 h R \\ \Rightarrow & d=\sqrt{2(500)\left(6.4 \times 10^6\right)}=80 \mathrm{~km} \end{array}$

subject: Physics
q: If the highest frequency modulating a carrier is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>, then the number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> broadcast stations accommodated in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> bandwidth are
question: If the highest frequency modulating a carrier is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>, then the number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> broadcast stations accommodated in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> bandwidth are
solution:
B. W. (Bandwidth) =2× maximum frequency at modulating signal

=2×5 kHz

=10 kHz

∴ No of stations accommodate =9010=9

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : A speech signal of 2 kHz is used to modulate a carrier signal of 1 MHz. The bandwidth requirement for the signal is 4 kHz.

Statement II : The side band frequencies are 1002 kHz and 998 kHz. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : A speech signal of 2 kHz is used to modulate a carrier signal of 1 MHz. The bandwidth requirement for the signal is 4 kHz.

Statement II : The side band frequencies are 1002 kHz and 998 kHz. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
fm=2 kHz

fc=1 MHz=1000 kHz

Band width =2fm=4 kHz

∴ Side frequencies will be

=fc±fm

=1000±2 kHz

=998 kHz & 1002 kHz

So, statement-I & statement-II both are correct.

subject: Physics
q: The height of transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the height of the receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>245</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight will be: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
question: The height of transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the height of the receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>245</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight will be: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
solution:
The maximum distance for line of sight communication is 

d=2hR+2h'R

The given data is 

R=6400 kmh=180 m

h'=245 m

Thus, the value is

d=2×180×6400×103

+2×245×6400×103

=6×80×102+7×80×102

=104 km

subject: Physics
q: A $10 \mathrm{~kW}$ transmitter emits radio waves of wavelength $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The number of photons emitted per second by the transmitter is of the order of
question: A $10 \mathrm{~kW}$ transmitter emits radio waves of wavelength $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The number of photons emitted per second by the transmitter is of the order of
solution:
Power $=\frac{n h c}{\lambda}$
(where, $n=$ no. of photons per second)
$$
\Rightarrow \quad n=\frac{10 \times 10^3 \times 500}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8} \simeq 10^{31}
$$

subject: Physics
q: What should be the height of transmitting antenna and the population covered if the television telecast is to cover a radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>?</mo></math>  The average population density around the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: What should be the height of transmitting antenna and the population covered if the television telecast is to cover a radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>?</mo></math>  The average population density around the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Radius covered, r=2RHT
150 km=2×6.5×106 mHT
150 km×1032=2×6.5×106HT
HT=1731 m

Population covered =πr22000 km-2
=3.14×(150)2×2000=1413×105
